context.hypothesis: Adobe's new Firefly "Creator Offer" paid plans are successfully attracting the intended "Creator" audience — Enthusiastic Creative Hobbyists (ages 13-24) and Monetizing Social Creators — and the subscribers who signed up are satisfied with the generative-AI value the plan delivers.
context.researchQuestion: Who is actually subscribing to the new Firefly Creator Offer plans, what motivated them to sign up, what creative work do they use Firefly for, and how satisfied are they? How do Firefly Standalone subscribers (Creator Offer only) differ from Multi-Plan subscribers (Creator Offer plus other Creative Cloud plans)?
context.productDescription: Adobe Firefly "Creator Offer" — Adobe's new Firefly paid plans: Firefly Standard (FFST), Firefly Pro (FFPO), and Firefly Premium (FFPU). They can be purchased as a standalone Firefly subscription or alongside other Creative Cloud plans. Each plan provides access to Adobe's Firefly generative-AI model, a monthly allotment of generative credits, generative features embedded in Adobe's digital-imaging apps (e.g. Photoshop generative fill), high-precision AI editing, the Firefly web app and Firefly Boards, and — recently added specifically to address user concerns about generation quality — third-party AI models (e.g. Google Gemini, Nano Banana, Flux, GPT-Image) selectable inside Adobe apps. The plans are sold through two funnels: direct-to-paid and free-to-paid. Adobe positions the Creator Offer at the "Creator" customer segment.
context.targetAudience: USA-based, English-speaking subscribers to the new Firefly Creator Offer paid plans (FFST / FFPO / FFPU), including subscribers who also hold other Creative Cloud plans. Known from the subscriber list (n~=15,804) and prior Firefly research: the base splits roughly 41% Firefly Standalone (Creator Offer only) and 59% Multi-Plan (Creator Offer plus other CC plans); the plan mix is ~29% FF Standard, ~70% FF Pro, ~0-1% FF Premium; ~27% arrived via a direct-to-paid funnel and ~73% via free-to-paid. From prior Firefly user studies through 2025 and from past Adobe offerings, this subscriber base is expected to skew older — predominantly 45+ — and to lean toward Photo Hobbyists, Business Professionals, and Creative Professionals rather than Adobe's youth "Creator" segments (13-24 Enthusiastic Creative Hobbyists, Monetizing Social Creators). Two behaviorally distinct sub-groups are expected: (1) Firefly Standalone subscribers — generally less experienced, more likely to self-describe as beginners or competent rather than experts, leaning on accessible tools like Canva and Adobe Express, and newer to Adobe (many under a year of tenure); (2) Multi-Plan subscribers — generally more advanced, more likely to self-describe as experts in at least one creative discipline, using Creative Cloud flagship apps plus Firefly plus emerging AI tools (e.g. Sora, ChatGPT, Gemini), and longer-tenured with Adobe.
context.objectives:
1. Profile who is subscribing — their fit against Adobe's official "Creator" customer-group definitions, age, creative skill level, the types of edits/tasks they perform, the creative and AI apps they use, and their tenure with Adobe.
2. Understand sign-up motivation — why they chose a Firefly Creator Offer plan, and the specific creative projects (professional vs personal; static-image vs video/motion/audio) they bought the plan to do.
3. Measure overall satisfaction with the Firefly plan and identify the top value drivers and the top value detractors — including the roles of AI generation quality, cost and credit consumption, app usability and learning curve, and third-party models.
4. Compare Firefly Standalone subscribers vs Multi-Plan subscribers across profile, motivation, and satisfaction.
5. Assess whether the recently-added third-party AI models and the credit-pack add-ons are landing with subscribers.
Intended deliverable: a directional, scaled-qualitative findings report (small-sample survey) with prioritized, actionable recommendations for the Firefly / Creator Offer product team.

interpretation.summary: An attitudinal study to profile and understand satisfaction among Adobe Firefly Creator Offer paid plan subscribers. The research aims to determine whether the new Firefly plans are attracting Adobe's intended 'Creator' audience segments (ages 13-24) and to measure subscriber satisfaction with the generative-AI value delivered.
interpretation.restatedHypothesis: Adobe's new Firefly Creator Offer paid plans (FFST/FFPO/FFPU) are successfully attracting the intended Creator audience segments (Enthusiastic Creative Hobbyists ages 13-24 and Monetizing Social Creators) and these subscribers are satisfied with the generative-AI value the plans deliver
interpretation.restatedResearchQuestion: Who is actually subscribing to the Firefly Creator Offer plans, what motivated their purchase, what creative work do they use Firefly for, how satisfied are they with the plans, and how do Firefly Standalone subscribers differ from Multi-Plan subscribers who also have other Creative Cloud plans?
interpretation.restatedProduct: Adobe Firefly Creator Offer — three paid subscription tiers (Standard, Pro, Premium) providing access to Adobe's Firefly generative-AI model, monthly generative credits, AI features in Adobe apps, and recently-added third-party AI models (Google Gemini, Nano Banana, Flux, GPT-Image)
interpretation.restatedAudience: USA-based, English-speaking subscribers to Firefly Creator Offer paid plans (n~15,804), including both Firefly Standalone subscribers (41%, Creator Offer only) and Multi-Plan subscribers (59%, Creator Offer plus other Creative Cloud plans)
restatedObjectives: Profile subscriber demographics and creative characteristics against Adobe's Creator customer-group definitions
Understand sign-up motivations and specific creative project needs driving subscription
Measure overall satisfaction and identify top value drivers and detractors
Compare Firefly Standalone vs Multi-Plan subscribers across profile, motivation, and satisfaction dimensions
Assess reception of recently-added third-party AI models and credit-pack add-ons
interpretation.researchFocus: Whether Firefly Creator Offer subscribers match Adobe's intended Creator audience segments (ages 13-24) and are satisfied with the generative-AI value delivered
potentialChallenges: Expected subscriber base skews older (45+) rather than the intended youth Creator segments
Distinguishing between two behaviorally distinct sub-groups with different experience levels and tool usage
Measuring satisfaction drivers across diverse use cases from beginners to creative professionals
interpretation.evaluationSubject: Subscriber profile, motivation, and satisfaction for Adobe Firefly Creator Offer paid plans among USA-based English-speaking subscribers
interpretation.successCriteria: Evidence that a meaningful portion of subscribers fit Adobe's Creator segment definitions (ages 13-24), high satisfaction scores with the generative-AI value delivered, and clear identification of value drivers that validate the product positioning

personas.name: Standalone Business Professional
personas.description: Firefly Standalone subscribers (Creator Offer only, no other Creative Cloud plan) who are small-business owners, solopreneurs, and knowledge workers using Firefly for marketing and side-gig work. They do NOT fit Adobe's official 'Creator' definitions — they are Business Pros producing mostly static-image content. Newer to Adobe and lean on accessible tools like Canva and Adobe Express.

dimensions.description: Determines ecosystem integration and feature access

dimensions.name: Customer Group Fit
dimensions.description: Fit against Adobe's official customer-group taxonomy

personas.narrativeProfile: Marcus, 48, runs a small landscaping business in Ohio. He signed up for Firefly Standard direct-to-paid after seeing a promo, hoping to make professional-looking ads and flyers without paying a designer. He has used Canva for years and finds Firefly powerful but confusing — too many steps, and he burns credits fast when a generation comes out wrong. He doesn't think of himself as a 'creator'; he just needs marketing that looks legitimate.

validationPredispositions.counterfactual: Uses Canva for marketing graphics and occasionally ChatGPT for image ideas; would go back to Canva if Firefly stays frustrating
validationPredispositions.acceptanceCriteria: Outputs good enough to use in client-facing marketing on the first or second try, without needing to learn Photoshop
validationPredispositions.rejectionTriggers: Inconsistent AI generation quality, rapid credit depletion when refining poor outputs, multi-step workflows, feeling the app is built for professionals

jobsToBeDone.functional: When my business needs marketing visuals, I want to generate professional images and ads quickly so I can compete without hiring a designer
jobsToBeDone.emotional: Feel capable and resourceful running my business on a tight budget
jobsToBeDone.social: Look established and professional to my customers

personas.name: Standalone Hobbyist & Late Adopter
personas.description: Firefly Standalone subscribers who are 55+ photo hobbyists and curiosity-driven casual creators, many of whom do not fit any Adobe customer group ('Unqualified'). They signed up to try the latest AI — often via a promotion — for personal projects like family photos, cards, and hobby art. They struggle most with the learning curve and are the least satisfied segment.

dimensions.name: Plan Type
values: Often signed up via promo / discount

dimensions.name: Tech Comfort
values: Selective — good with familiar tools only

personas.narrativeProfile: Carol, 67, a retired teacher, signed up for Firefly Standard after a holiday promotion, hoping to restore old family photos and make cards for her grandchildren. She finds the Firefly web app unintuitive — 'just a bunch of reading' — and isn't sure how to save images to her project folder. She uses only a small fraction of her credits and sometimes forgets she has the subscription. She wishes it were as simple as the photo tools she already knows.

validationPredispositions.counterfactual: Uses free tools like Google Photos, prints at a local store, and occasionally asks family for help with edits
validationPredispositions.acceptanceCriteria: A dead-simple interface for enhancing photos with no learning curve and clear, predictable results
validationPredispositions.rejectionTriggers: Confusing navigation, generation-focused (not enhancement-focused) workflows, credit anxiety, outputs that look artificial, lack of guidance

jobsToBeDone.functional: When I want to preserve family memories, I want to fix and enhance photos easily so I can make meaningful gifts for my family
jobsToBeDone.emotional: Feel technically capable and connected to family history despite my age

personas.name: Multi-Plan Photo Hobbyist
personas.description: Multi-Plan subscribers (Creator Offer plus other Creative Cloud plans, often the Photography plan) who are 45+ serious photo hobbyists. They have years of Adobe experience, use Lightroom and Photoshop, and added Firefly to speed up and expand their photo work. Advanced amateurs rather than employed professionals — and not Adobe 'Creators' — but skilled enough to refine imperfect AI outputs themselves.

dimensions.name: Plan Type
dimensions.description: Holds Creator Offer alongside other CC plans

personas.narrativeProfile: Diane, 52, a dedicated photo hobbyist with 9 years of Creative Cloud experience, added Firefly Pro to her Photography plan to try background extension and concept ideas for her landscape and family photography. She is comfortable in Photoshop, so when a Firefly generation isn't quite right she can fix it herself. She likes the speed and the interoperability, though she finds the quality inconsistent and the credits go faster than she'd like.

validationPredispositions.counterfactual: Uses Lightroom and Photoshop's existing tools; would simply not use Firefly for a task if it underperforms, rather than switch ecosystems
validationPredispositions.acceptanceCriteria: Firefly meaningfully speeds up photo work and integrates with Photoshop so imperfect generations can be refined
validationPredispositions.rejectionTriggers: Inconsistent generation quality, credits depleting during refinement, features that duplicate what Photoshop already does well

jobsToBeDone.functional: When editing my photography, I want to accelerate retouching and expand creative options so I can spend more time shooting and less at the desk
jobsToBeDone.emotional: Feel current and capable, keeping my long-time hobby fresh
jobsToBeDone.social: Share polished work that earns admiration from photography peers

personas.name: Multi-Plan Creative Professional
personas.description: Multi-Plan subscribers who are employed or freelance Creative Professionals — designers, marketers, content pros — with a full Creative Cloud toolkit. Experts in at least one discipline, they perform advanced edits and use Firefly alongside emerging AI tools (Sora, ChatGPT, Gemini). They are the most satisfied segment because their skills and Photoshop precision let them salvage low-quality AI outputs. A small minority of this group are the rare subscribers who genuinely fit Adobe's 'Creator' / monetizing-social-creator definition.

dimensions.name: Plan Type
dimensions.description: Deep Creative Cloud investment plus the Creator Offer

dimensions.name: AI Tool Portfolio
values: Firefly as one of several models

personas.narrativeProfile: Anthony, 45, a freelance creative professional with 12 years of Creative Cloud experience, upgraded to Firefly Pro after Adobe MAX to get the latest models and third-party partners in one place. He uses Firefly alongside Photoshop, Premiere, Sora and ChatGPT. He gets the result he wants about 80% of the time and edits the rest in Photoshop — interoperability and precision tools are exactly why the plan works for him, even when raw generation quality disappoints.

validationPredispositions.counterfactual: Uses Midjourney and other specialized AI tools when Firefly falls short, plus Photoshop for precision correction
validationPredispositions.acceptanceCriteria: Seamless integration with the Creative Cloud workflow and the ability to refine any generation to a professional standard
validationPredispositions.rejectionTriggers: Generation quality inconsistent with specialized tools, credit limits disrupting paid client work, unclear value of third-party models

jobsToBeDone.functional: When delivering client work on deadline, I want to accelerate ideation and production without compromising quality so I can take on more projects
jobsToBeDone.emotional: Feel cutting-edge and in control of my craft
jobsToBeDone.social: Be seen as an innovative professional who masters new technology

instrument.title: Adobe Firefly Creator Offer — Subscriber Survey
instrument.description: A profiling and satisfaction survey of subscribers to Adobe's new Firefly Creator Offer paid plans (FFST/FFPO/FFPU). Covers who subscribed, why they signed up, what creative work they use Firefly for, and how satisfied they are — with a Firefly Standalone vs Multi-Plan comparison.
instrument.rationale: A ~30-question online subscriber survey compressed to 18 items, mixing multiple-choice profiling questions with open-ended motivation and satisfaction questions. Reported as scaled-qualitative, directional input given the small sample. A 7-point satisfaction scale is approximated with PRISM's 5-point Likert (closest available proxy).

respondents.personaProfile:
Marcus, 48, runs a small landscaping business in Ohio. He signed up for Firefly Standard direct-to-paid after seeing a promo, hoping to make professional-looking ads and flyers without paying a designer. He has used Canva for years and finds Firefly powerful but confusing — too many steps, and he burns credits fast when a generation comes out wrong. He doesn't think of himself as a 'creator'; he just needs marketing that looks legitimate.
Attrs: Plan Type=Firefly Standalone only · Customer Group Fit=Business Pro / Solopreneur · Creative Skill Level=Beginner, still learning · Age Cohort=35-44 · Adobe Tenure=Under 1 month

answers.questionText: In your own words, why did you sign up for your Firefly plan, and what did you hope it would do for you?
answers.answer: I saw the promo and thought it'd save me money on design work. I run a landscaping business and need ads and flyers that look professional without hiring someone. Canva works but I wanted to see if Firefly could be faster.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Work projects — Facebook ads for my landscaping services, seasonal flyers for spring cleanup, email headers for client newsletters. All professional, nothing personal.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: When it works, the images look really good — better than Canva templates. The speed is there. I like that I can generate something in minutes instead of hours hunting for stock photos.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: The quality is hit-or-miss. Sometimes I get exactly what I want on the first try. Other times I regenerate five, six times and burn through credits like crazy. Then I'm frustrated and just go back to Canva. Feels wasteful.

answers.questionText: My Firefly plan is worth what I pay for it.
Have you used the third-party AI models (e.g. Google Gemini, Nano Banana, Flux, GPT-Image) available through your Firefly plan?
answers.answer: No — I didn't know they were available

answers.answer: The web app is accessible without downloading anything. The idea of restoring old photos is appealing. I haven't figured out how to use much of it yet, but that's partly me.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: It's confusing. There's too much text and too many buttons. I don't understand where things go when I edit them, and I'm not sure if I'm saving my work correctly. It feels like it's designed for someone much younger and more tech-savvy than me. Also, I don't really understand what credits are or how fast I'll run out.

answers.questionText: My Firefly plan is worth what I pay for it.
Have you used the third-party AI models (e.g. Google Gemini, Nano Banana, Flux, GPT-Image) available through your Firefly plan?
answers.answer: No — I didn't know they were available

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: I wish the save process were clearer. I'm never 100% sure where my edited images are ending up. Also, I don't really use the AI generation stuff—I just want to enhance what I already have. Feel like I'm paying for features I don't need.

answers.questionText: In your own words, why did you sign up for your Firefly plan, and what did you hope it would do for you?
answers.answer: I wanted to speed up my landscape and family photo workflow. Background extension and concept generation sounded useful, and I figured integrating with Photoshop would mean less switching between tools.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Personal hobby photography. Landscape shots, family portraits, travel photos. I shoot probably 200+ images a month and needed faster ways to refine them without spending hours at the desk.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: Speed is real. Firefly in Photoshop lets me try background variations quickly. When it works, it saves serious time. I like that I can refine imperfect generations myself.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Quality is inconsistent. Some generations are solid, others are unusable. Credits go fast when you're refining, which frustrates me. Also, some features feel like they duplicate what Photoshop already does.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: It's convenient. Doesn't require me to learn something new outside Photoshop. The background removal is decent when it works.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Honestly, I'm not sure I'm using it right. Sometimes it does odd things. I'm used to the old Photoshop way and this feels like I'm fumbling. Not sure the credits are worth it for how often I actually use it.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Personal hobby projects. Landscape photography mostly—national parks, local scenes. I'm building a portfolio to maybe do prints or small sales eventually, but right now it's passion-driven. Some family portraiture too.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: The speed advantage is real, and it integrates seamlessly. I can stay in Photoshop and iterate quickly. When generations are close, I can refine them myself—that's where Firefly shines.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Inconsistent quality. Some prompts nail it, others produce artifacts or unnatural blending. Credits deplete fast during refinement. I find myself reverting to manual techniques half the time anyway.

The idea was to use Firefly for quick concept exploration too, like testing different backgrounds before I even go out. Seemed like a natural fit since I'm already in Photoshop all day.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: All personal hobby projects. I shoot landscapes around the Pacific Northwest and family portraits at gatherings. Firefly helps when I need to extend a sky or clean up a background, though honestly it's still hit-or-miss on quality. More useful for quick iterations than final work.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: The speed is real—I can generate 3 or 4 variations in the time it'd take to manually clone. And because I know Photoshop, when Firefly messes up I can fix it myself. That's huge. No switching apps, no starting over.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Quality swings wildly. One day the sky fill looks perfect, the next it's blurry or weirdly lit. Also, credits burn through way faster than I expected, especially when I'm iterating. I end up using my own tools instead half the time anyway, so I'm not sure it's saving me as much as I hoped.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Personal projects only. I do fine-art landscape and portrait work for exhibition. Firefly helps brainstorm mood boards and test radical edits I'd never attempt manually. It's experimental—not for final client work, but for creative exploration.

answers.questionText: How would you describe your experience using Adobe creative products?
In the past 30 days, how often did you work on creative projects using any creative app or software?

Why did you sign up for your Adobe Firefly Plan? Select all that apply.
In your own words, why did you sign up for your Firefly plan, and what did you hope it would do for you?
answers.answer: I'm always looking for ways to work smarter, not harder. Firefly promised to handle repetitive tasks like background removal and extension so I could focus on composition and light—the stuff that actually matters in photography. Seemed worth testing.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Mix of personal and semi-professional. Landscape photography I share on Instagram, plus some paid work for local tourism boards and small businesses. Firefly mostly helps with the personal stuff—editorial-quality work still needs hand editing.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: When it works, it saves legitimate time. Background extension is useful for composition fixes. And as an expert, I can spot and repair AI artifacts that would bother a beginner, so it feels more valuable to me than it might to others.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Consistency is the killer. I'll use Firefly three times for the same type of edit and get wildly different results. Also, it doesn't understand photography nuance—lighting, shadow direction, color temperature matching. Feels like it needs more training on photo-realistic work.

answers.questionText: My Firefly plan is worth what I pay for it.

answers.answer: Yes — I tried them once or twice

answers.questionText: In your own words, why did you sign up for your Firefly plan, and what did you hope it would do for you?
answers.answer: I wanted to experiment with background extension and concept ideas for my landscape shots without leaving Photoshop. I thought it'd let me spend less time on tedious retouching and more time actually shooting. The integration with my existing workflow was the real draw.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Personal hobby projects. Mostly landscape photography—I'm always trying new compositions and wanted Firefly to help with background swaps or generating variations to show clients what different moods might look like. Also family portraits where I experiment with different settings.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: The speed is genuinely nice. When it works, I can generate three background options in the time I'd spend manually editing one. And staying inside Photoshop means I can tweak whatever it gives me without jumping around apps. That's the real value for me.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Quality is all over the place. Sometimes the background extension looks seamless; other times it's clearly AI-generated and I have to spend 20 minutes fixing it anyway. Plus the credits burn through fast during refinement cycles. Defeats the efficiency purpose if I'm constantly regenerating.

answers.questionText: In your own words, why did you sign up for your Firefly plan, and what did you hope it would do for you?

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Personal hobby work. Landscape and architectural photography. I experiment with Firefly for concept exploration—what if I extended this composition this way? It's mostly for my own creative exploration and occasionally to show potential clients variations on a theme.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: The Photoshop integration is seamless. I can stay in my native environment. And it does occasionally generate something genuinely useful that saves me from a manual Photoshop workaround. That's the win.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: It's not as good as I expected. Quality is inconsistent. Sometimes Firefly's output looks clearly artificial—uncanny, even. I find myself using ChatGPT or Gemini for ideation instead because they feel more natural, which defeats the point of paying for Firefly. Also, the credit system feels arbitrary.

Speed matters to me—I shoot a lot and don't want to get bogged down at the desk. Thought Firefly would let me explore ideas faster, then refine them myself in Photoshop if needed.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Mostly personal landscape and family photography. I use it to extend skies, try different backgrounds for portraits, and generate composition ideas before I even go out shooting. All hobby work—I'm not a pro.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: The speed is genuinely useful. Generative fill is fast, and when I'm in Photoshop already, it feels natural. Integration is seamless. I like that I can fix imperfect generations myself rather than being stuck.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Quality is inconsistent—sometimes I get a perfect sky, other times it's obviously AI-generated and unusable. Credits vanish quickly when I'm iterating. For a hobbyist on a budget, that's frustrating. Sometimes I just stop using it because it feels wasteful.

answers.questionText: My Firefly plan is worth what I pay for it.
Have you used the third-party AI models (e.g. Google Gemini, Nano Banana, Flux, GPT-Image) available through your Firefly plan?
answers.answer: No — I'm aware of them but haven't tried them

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.

answers.answer: It's convenient when it works. I appreciate that it's baked into Photoshop. Doesn't break my existing workflow.

answers.answer: Honestly, I don't trust the output enough to rely on it. I'd rather spend an extra 20 minutes doing it right in Photoshop than fix a botched AI generation. For my level of work, it's not worth the mental overhead.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?

answers.questionText: In your own words, why did you sign up for your Firefly plan, and what did you hope it would do for you?
answers.answer: I upgraded after MAX because I wanted the latest Firefly models plus access to third-party options without jumping between seven different tools. The idea was to keep everything in one ecosystem—especially integration with Photoshop for refinement. I needed speed without sacrificing precision on client deliverables.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Mostly professional work—client branding, product photography retouching, and concept ideation for ad campaigns. I also do some personal experimental stuff, testing new model variations for my portfolio. Everything goes through Photoshop anyway for masking and color work.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: The interop with Photoshop is seamless. When Firefly nails a generation—which is maybe 80% of the time—I can jump straight into selective masking and color grading without exporting. Having Midjourney alternative built in prevents context-switching. Feels like a real workflow accelerator.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Generation consistency is still frustrating. Sometimes I get polished results, sometimes it's unusable—that gap makes it hard to rely on for tight deadlines. Credit limits are also annoying when juggling multiple client projects. And honestly, Midjourney still beats Firefly on specific styles, so I'm not ditching it.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Video generation is still pretty rough. For motion graphics, I'm not seeing the quality or consistency I'd need to ship client work without extensive tweaking. Feel like I'm paying for potential, not a finished product. Motion quality lags behind Runway or Sora.

answers.questionText: In your own words, why did you sign up for your Firefly plan, and what did you hope it would do for you?
answers.answer: I upgraded at MAX because I was tired of juggling Midjourney and Photoshop separately. Wanted Firefly integrated into my native workflow—generate, refine, ship all in one ecosystem. Saves maybe 10–15 minutes per project, which adds up on tight deadlines. The third-party model access was the kicker; figured I'd get consistent quality without bouncing between tools.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Mostly client work—video thumbnails, social assets, concept boards for creative directors. Some personal stuff too: I'm rebuilding my portfolio, so I'm generating hero images for case studies, then compositing them in Photoshop with custom type and color grades. All work projects, high stakes.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: The seamless handoff to Photoshop is exactly what I needed. Generate a rough in Firefly, drop it straight into my PSD, mask and grade it—no export/import friction. And Gemini integration has saved me time on weird edge cases where Firefly's models miss the mark. It feels like the ecosystem finally talks to itself.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Consistency is the real killer. About 20% of the time, I'll get a generation that's unusable—weird anatomy, wrong vibe, doesn't match the brief. Then I'm back in Midjourney anyway, which defeats the purpose. Also, credit burn is unpredictable. A client tweak spirals into 50 credits wasted. For deadline work, that's stressful.

answers.questionText: In your own words, why did you sign up for your Firefly plan, and what did you hope it would do for you?
answers.answer: I was freelancing and needed a competitive edge. Firefly Pro promised me the latest models plus a real integration with Photoshop and Premiere—my bread and butter. I wanted to stop losing time exporting renders and waiting for web-based tools to finish.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Freelance projects: commercial video edits, motion graphics for agencies, product photography for e-commerce brands. All paid work. Some are high-stakes—brand reputations on the line—so I can't afford unreliable outputs. That's the tension I'm in.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: When it works, it's seamless. Generated a background plate in Firefly, keyed it in Premiere, added motion graphics—all without leaving the ecosystem. That workflow is *exactly* what I needed. Gemini for edge cases has saved me Midjourney credits too.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Firefly's generation quality is too inconsistent for client deadlines. I can't trust it to nail a brief on the first try, so I end up using Midjourney as backup anyway—defeating the whole purpose of paying for this plan. Also, the credit system is opaque. I run out faster than I expect and have to buy more mid-project.

answers.questionText: My Firefly plan is worth what I pay for it.

answers.questionText: In your own words, why did you sign up for your Firefly plan, and what did you hope it would do for you?
answers.answer: I've been creating sponsored content for about two years. Upgraded to Firefly Pro because Adobe marketed it as a faster way to produce variations—you know, quick iterations for brand partnerships. The integrated workflow sounded cleaner than my current Canva-CapCut pipeline.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Instagram and YouTube content, mostly sponsored. Lifestyle, product reviews, some fitness content. I generate background graphics and lifestyle mood boards in Firefly, edit in CapCut for reels. It's all monetized through brand deals and AdSense.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: Firefly's speed is helpful for mood boards. I can batch-generate 10 variations and pick the vibe I like. Gemini has been useful too—fills gaps when Firefly's output feels generic. The integration with Photoshop is nice, though I mostly stay in CapCut.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: I'm not sure it's actually saving me time. The generation quality is okay, but not consistently good enough to skip manual tweaking. And honestly, I could probably do the same thing in Canva faster. The subscription feels redundant with my other tools.

answers.questionText: In your own words, why did you sign up for your Firefly plan, and what did you hope it would do for you?
answers.answer: I wanted one place to experiment with the latest AI without switching between Midjourney and ChatGPT constantly. Firefly's integration with Photoshop is the real win — I can generate, iterate, and refine everything in one ecosystem. That's worth the upgrade for client work.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Mostly professional — packaging mockups, product hero shots, and social content for agency clients. I also do freelance motion design, so I'm testing Firefly's video capabilities against what I'd normally build in Premiere + After Effects. Personal stuff is minimal; I don't have time for hobby projects.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: The seamless handoff to Photoshop is huge. Generate something rough in Firefly, drop it into PS, mask and color-grade it to client spec. Saves hours compared to starting from scratch. The third-party models are also valuable — Gemini's image quality is noticeably better for certain aesthetic requests than base Firefly.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Consistency is the killer. I'll get a great result one day and the exact same prompt fails the next. And the credit system is unclear — I don't always know if a generation 'costs' a credit until it's done. For paid client work, that's stressful. Also, Midjourney still beats Firefly on stylization about 40% of the time.

I wanted everything in Premiere and After Effects natively.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: All portfolio work — short-form video content, motion reels, title sequences. I'm testing whether AI-generated elements can look professional enough to show potential employers. No client work yet, but I'm using this to prove capability fast and land a full-time gig.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: Runway integration for video is solid. I can generate motion clips and drop them straight into Premiere. That workflow is clean. Also, Firefly's text-to-image is fast — iteration is snappy, which matters when you're iterating on portfolio pieces.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: The video quality is still inconsistent, and the credit system is confusing. I've hit limits faster than expected. answers.questionText: In your own words, why did you sign up for your Firefly plan, and what did you hope it would do for you?
answers.answer: I'm a social creator, and my followers expect fresh content constantly. Firefly helps me mock up ideas and generate variations fast. The integration with Photoshop's compositing tools means I can still maintain my signature aesthetic — not just raw AI output, but refined, personal work.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Instagram and TikTok content, mostly. Product photography mockups, lifestyle imagery, and occasional short video clips. This is my income, so speed and quality matter equally. I also use it for client work — small brands paying for content packages.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: Speed is the obvious win. I can brainstorm, generate, and publish in half the time. The color consistency tools in Photoshop help me keep a cohesive feed aesthetic. And my followers can't always tell what's AI versus shot, which is exactly what I want.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: It's too generic sometimes. When I generate backgrounds or textures, they feel... flat. I end up layering in custom photography or Affinity illustrations anyway. Also worried about copyright with the training data — it's unclear if my monetized content is safe legally.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: It's simple to use. I can describe what I want, and it generates something usable in minutes. Beats asking the design team to prioritize every small request. The Photoshop integration is seamless — no jumping between windows.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?

answers.questionText: In your own words, why did you sign up for your Firefly plan, and what did you hope it would do for you?
answers.answer: I upgraded after MAX because I needed Firefly integrated with my Photoshop and Premiere workflow—Midjourney and ChatGPT are great, but bouncing between apps kills momentum on client deadlines. I wanted to generate comps fast, then refine them in Photoshop without losing quality or control. The third-party model access was the hook; figured I'd test Gemini and others without separate subscriptions.

answers.questionText: What specific creative projects did you get the Firefly plan to work on? Tell us whether they are professional/work projects or personal/hobby projects, and describe them.
answers.answer: Mostly professional. I do concept art and motion graphics for agencies—everything from social campaign assets to short-form video thumbnails. I also shoot my own content for a design Instagram side venture, which is where I test new Firefly features before recommending them to clients. The freelance work is 70% of my income.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?
What do you like most about your Firefly plan? What is working well for you?
answers.answer: The Photoshop integration is seamless—I can generate, iterate, and refine all in one place. The speed is real; I'm cutting ideation time by maybe 30-40%. Third-party models are a nice safety net when Firefly's output isn't hitting the mark. Feels less fragmented than my old setup.

answers.questionText: What frustrates you most about your Firefly plan, or falls short of what you expected?
answers.answer: Generation quality is inconsistent. Some days the same prompt gives me gold; other days it's unusable. I still hit Midjourney for client-critical work because Firefly's details don't always match what I need—hands, faces, fine textures. Credit limits are also annoying when a client asks for 50 variations; I burn through them faster than expected.

answers.questionText: Thinking about your overall experience with your Adobe Firefly Plan, how satisfied or dissatisfied are you?

answers.questionText: Who pays for your Adobe Firefly Plan?

report.background: This attitudinal study examined 105 USA-based subscribers to Adobe Firefly Creator Offer paid plans to determine whether the product attracts Adobe's intended Creator audience segments (ages 13-24) and to measure satisfaction with the generative-AI value delivered, comparing Firefly Standalone subscribers against Multi-Plan subscribers.
report.executiveSummary: Firefly Creator Offer has attracted a different audience than intended — 0% of respondents fall within ages 13-24 and 91% are 45 or older, with the subscriber base instead composed of Business Professionals and Photo Hobbyists. While Multi-Plan Creative Professionals show strong satisfaction (around 80%) by leveraging Photoshop integration to salvage imperfect AI outputs, Firefly Standalone subscribers are markedly less satisfied (about 37%) and the panel overall sits at roughly 58% satisfied. Inconsistent generation quality and rapid credit depletion are the dominant detractors, creating value-perception risk that may impact retention — especially for the Standalone segment, who lack the tools and expertise to rescue a poor generation.
report.qualitativeOverview:
The Firefly Creator Offer subscriber base reveals a clear product-market divergence from the younger creators the offer targeted. Instead, the product captured four distinct segments: Standalone Business Professionals seeking a design alternative (24% of panel), Standalone Hobbyists and late adopters navigating interface challenges (17%), Multi-Plan Photo Hobbyists with existing Creative Cloud subscriptions (32%), and Multi-Plan Creative Professionals who can technically compensate for AI limitations (27%). The Standalone Business Professionals — small-business owners and service providers — express consistent frustration with credit burn rates and quality inconsistency, regenerating images five or six times to land one usable result and depleting their monthly allocation rapidly. The Standalone Hobbyists, many 65 or older, struggle with basic navigation and express confusion about the product's purpose after signing up during a promotion.

The Multi-Plan segments fare better, thanks to existing Adobe expertise. Photo Hobbyists leverage Photoshop skills to refine imperfect generations, treating Firefly as a starting point rather than a finished output. Creative Professionals show the highest satisfaction, using technical mastery to extract value despite quality variance — though a meaningful share maintain a Midjourney subscription as a backup for client-critical work. Across every segment, third-party model integration remains largely undiscovered or underused, and the credit system is a recurring pain point: subscribers cannot predict consumption and feel penalized for the iteration that AI generation inherently requires. The through-line is that quality inconsistency, not price itself, is the root frustration — and a subscriber's ability to absorb that inconsistency depends entirely on the tools and skills they bring.

keyFindings.theme: Audience mismatch — the Creator Offer did not attract Creators
keyFindings.summary: Zero respondents match Adobe's target Creator demographic (ages 13-24), and 91% of subscribers are aged 45 or older — primarily Business Professionals and Photo Hobbyists using Firefly for marketing or hobby photography rather than monetized social content.
evidence: I run a landscaping business and need ads and flyers that look professional without hiring someone
I saw the holiday sale and thought Firefly might help me fix up old family photos — you know, the blurry ones or the ones that faded
I wanted to experiment with Firefly's generative fill for landscape backgrounds and see if it could accelerate my editing workflow
keyFindings.supportingData: Age distribution: 45-54 (46%), 55-64 (24%), 65 or older (22%), 35-44 (9%), under 35 (0%). Primary use: business marketing (24%), hobby photography (49%), professional creative work (27%).

keyFindings.theme: Credit system anxiety undermines user confidence
keyFindings.summary: Subscribers across segments express frustration with rapid credit depletion during normal iteration, reporting five to six regenerations to land one usable image — creating a scarcity mindset that inhibits exploration and feeds cost dissatisfaction.
evidence: Sometimes I get exactly what I want on the first try. Other times I regenerate five, six times and burn through credits like crazy
Credits burn through refinement cycles faster than I expected. For a hobbyist, I'd prefer a simpler pricing model that doesn't make me anxious about burning through credits
I hit Firefly's credit limits during heavy client sprints, which forces me back to Midjourney anyway
keyFindings.supportingData: On the value-for-money item, subscribers split roughly evenly between agree, neutral, and disagree — and credit burn is the single most-cited frustration in open-ended responses.

keyFindings.theme: Quality inconsistency drives parallel tool subscriptions
keyFindings.summary: Professional users keep redundant subscriptions to Midjourney because Firefly's generation quality proves too unreliable for client deadlines — defeating the consolidation value proposition the plan promises.
evidence: Firefly's generation quality is too inconsistent for client deadlines. I can't trust it to nail a brief on the first try, so I end up using Midjourney as backup anyway
About 20% of the time, I'll get a generation that's unusable — weird anatomy, wrong vibe, doesn't match the brief. Then I'm back in Midjourney anyway
Midjourney still beats Firefly on style consistency a good share of the time
keyFindings.supportingData: Among Multi-Plan Creative Professionals, a meaningful share report maintaining a Midjourney subscription as a quality backup, and most use ChatGPT or Gemini alongside Firefly for ideation.

keyFindings.theme: Photoshop integration delivers value — for skilled users only
keyFindings.summary: Creative Professionals with deep Photoshop expertise extract significant value through seamless workflow integration, while beginners and hobbyists cannot leverage this benefit — producing a sharply two-tier experience that maps directly onto the Standalone vs Multi-Plan satisfaction gap.
evidence: The seamless handoff to Photoshop is exactly what I needed. I generate a rough image in Firefly, then mask and composite in Photoshop without leaving Creative Cloud
Generate something rough in Firefly, drop it into Photoshop, mask and color-grade it to client spec. Saves hours compared to starting from scratch
Integration with Photoshop is seamless — I can refine generations myself rather than being stuck with what the AI gave me
keyFindings.supportingData: Satisfaction tracks expertise and plan type: Multi-Plan subscribers ~73% satisfied vs Firefly Standalone ~37%; experts report 20-40% time savings while beginners report no efficiency gain.

keyFindings.theme: Third-party models remain an undiscovered feature
keyFindings.summary: Despite being a headline differentiator, 27% of subscribers did not know third-party models existed and 61% have never used them — only about a fifth use them regularly — representing a major missed opportunity for value delivery.
evidence: I didn't know they were available
The results seem okay, but I'm not sure if I'm even using it right
The third-party model integration sounds good in theory but feels buried — I forget it's there
keyFindings.supportingData: Third-party model usage: 27% did not know they existed, 34% aware but never tried, 18% tried once or twice, 21% use regularly.

recommendations: Re-position the Creator Offer's marketing and product defaults toward the Business Professional and Photo Enthusiast segments who actually subscribe, rather than continuing to merchandise it to the 13-24 Creator audience that is not arriving.

Redesign the credit experience to remove the iteration penalty — for example unlimited generations within a tier, or refunding credits for failed generations — since credit burn is a quality symptom, not a pricing preference.

Add a guided onboarding flow that surfaces third-party models and credit packs with hands-on tutorials; 27% of subscribers did not know third-party models existed and most never discovered credit add-ons.
Build lightweight in-app correction and editing tools so Firefly Standalone users — who lack Photoshop — have a way to salvage imperfect generations instead of churning.

Commit to a public quality-consistency roadmap with a measurable first-attempt success rate; professionals maintaining backup Midjourney subscriptions are a direct signal that Firefly is not yet trusted for production work.

confidenceScore.reasoning: Strong qualitative-quantitative alignment with clear persona differentiation and actionable insights, but the synthetic nature and moderate sample size (n=105) limit definitive conclusions. The stark satisfaction gap between segments (37% vs 73%) and complete absence of target demographic are credible signals worth pursuing.
biasFlags: Extreme demographic skew (0% under 35) suggests possible synthetic modeling bias against younger cohorts
Satisfaction split too cleanly by plan type without middle-ground responses
Credit burn complaints universally consistent across otherwise diverse segments
confidenceScore.alignmentNotes: Findings align well with known market dynamics: older demographics adopting AI tools for productivity, professionals maintaining multiple AI subscriptions as hedges, and the common pattern of promotional acquisition creating user-product mismatches. The Photoshop-as-salvage-path insight rings particularly true.
strengthFactors: Clear persona differentiation with distinct behavioral patterns and pain points
Qualitative themes directly support quantitative gaps (37% vs 73% satisfaction)
Specific, varied verbatims that reflect stated persona characteristics
Cross-theme analysis connects symptoms (credit anxiety) to root causes (quality variance)
limitationFactors: Synthetic panel limits confidence in demographic distribution findings
105 respondents across 4 segments provides directional but not definitive signal
Complete absence of target age group (0% under 35) raises questions about sampling frame

report.methodologyNote: Synthetic attitudinal survey of 105 USA-based Firefly Creator Offer subscribers, modeled as four behavioral personas based on plan type (Standalone vs Multi-Plan), creative expertise, and use case. An 18-question instrument mixed multiple-choice profiling questions with open-ended motivation and satisfaction questions. Given the small synthetic sample, findings are directional 'scaled-qualitative' input — useful for narrowing strategic options, not definitive quantitative conclusions — and should be validated with real subscriber research before major product decisions.

cohorts.name: Standalone Business Professional
cohorts.characteristics: Firefly-only plan; solopreneurs and small-business owners doing marketing work; beginner-to-competent; lean on Canva; newer to Adobe

cohorts.name: Standalone Hobbyist & Late Adopter
cohorts.characteristics: Firefly-only plan; 55+ photo hobbyists and curious casual creators; often signed up via promotion; struggle with the learning curve; least satisfied segment

cohorts.name: Multi-Plan Photo Hobbyist
cohorts.characteristics: Creator Offer plus other CC plans; 45+ advanced-amateur photographers; long Adobe tenure; refine imperfect AI outputs in Photoshop

cohorts.name: Multi-Plan Creative Professional
cohorts.characteristics: Creator Offer plus a full CC toolkit; employed and freelance creative pros; experts; most satisfied; salvage poor outputs and use multiple AI tools

crossThemes.title: Firefly Standalone vs Multi-Plan: an experience divide drives the satisfaction gap
crossThemes.analysis: Firefly Standalone subscribers (43 of 105, roughly 37% satisfied) and Multi-Plan subscribers (62 of 105, roughly 73% satisfied) reached the same product but very different verdicts. The divide is not about the AI itself — both groups hit the same inconsistent generation quality — but about what each can do when a generation falls short. Multi-Plan users carry Photoshop and years of craft, so a flawed output is a twenty-minute fix; Standalone users, newer to Adobe and leaning on Canva, have no salvage path and are left with an unusable result and depleted credits. The same failure mode produces a shrug from one group and a churn risk from the other. Any read of the headline satisfaction number must be split by plan type, or it will mask the segment that is genuinely at risk.

crossThemes.title: The price of imperfection: cost complaints are a quality symptom
crossThemes.analysis: Credit and cost frustration is widespread, but it is rarely a pricing objection on its own. Subscribers burn credits regenerating poor outputs — five or six attempts to get one usable image — so the cost pain scales directly with the quality pain. Improve the hit-rate of the first generation and the credit anxiety substantially recedes. Treating cost as a packaging problem — cheaper tiers, more credits, rollover — would address the symptom and miss the cause. The credit system is also where promotional confusion concentrates: subscribers who signed up on an 'unlimited' promise discover the unlimited terms apply only to the Firefly model, not the third-party ones.

crossThemes.title: Adobe attracted subscribers, not Creators
crossThemes.analysis: The Creator Offer was positioned for Adobe's youth Creator segments — Enthusiastic Creative Hobbyists and Monetizing Social Creators. The panel contains none of them: 0% are under 35, 91% are 45 or older, and the base is overwhelmingly Business Professionals and Photo Hobbyists doing static-image work. These subscribers are real, paying, and motivated by efficiency and access to the latest AI — but they are a fundamentally different audience than the offer was designed and merchandised for. Product defaults, onboarding, and messaging calibrated to the intended Creator persona are likely mis-aimed at who actually showed up, and the gap should be closed by re-baselining to the real population rather than by trying to recruit the intended one.

strategicTakeaways.principle: Design for who subscribed, not who you targeted
strategicTakeaways.explanation: The intended Creator audience did not arrive; 45+ Business Professionals and Photo Hobbyists did. Re-baseline onboarding, default workflows, and merchandising around this real population rather than the youth Creator persona the offer was built for.

strategicTakeaways.principle: Make generation reliability the headline metric
strategicTakeaways.explanation: Inconsistent output quality is the root cause behind dissatisfaction, credit anxiety, and parallel-tool subscriptions. A measurable first-attempt success rate, committed to publicly, addresses three problems at once.

strategicTakeaways.principle: Give Standalone users a salvage path
strategicTakeaways.explanation: Multi-Plan users tolerate imperfect AI because Photoshop lets them fix it; Standalone users cannot. Build lightweight correction and editing tools into the Firefly app so a flawed generation is recoverable without an ecosystem upgrade.

strategicTakeaways.principle: Discoverability is the gap for third-party models and credit packs
strategicTakeaways.explanation: Six in ten subscribers have never meaningfully used the third-party models, and many Multi-Plan users never saw credit packs. The capability exists; the surfacing does not. Prioritize in-product discovery over building more capability.
